text:
Modeling pedestrians road crossing behavior in traﬃc
system micro-simulation in China
Jianguo Yang *, Wen Deng, Jinmei Wang, Qingfeng Li, Zhaoan Wang
School of Electrical Engineering, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China
Abstract
In many Chinese cities, pedestrians road crossing behavior is diﬀerent from that of pedestrians in developed countries.
This paper presents a pedestrian model for traﬃc system micro-simulation in China. Considering the high rate of signal
non-compliance, we classify pedestrians into two types: law-obeying ones and opportunistic ones. Opportunistic ones
decide whether to violate traﬃc signal during red man, depending on the states of some external factors (like policeman,
vehicle ﬂow and other pedestrians behaviors). Questionnaires were used to determine the proportions of these two types of
pedestrians under diﬀerent circumstances. In addition, a time gap distribution extracted from videotape were used to deter-
mine the criterion for pedestrians to decide whether to walk or wait when they conﬂict with vehicle ﬂows. However, sim-
ulation results deviate from the data extracted from videotape in some degree. By adjusting the parameters on the b

asis of
analyzing the occurrence of the deviations, the simulation results agree with the ﬁeld results better. This model has repre-
sented the high rate of pedestrians red light running and the mixed characteristics of traﬃc ﬂows in Chinese cities, and it
may be applicable in the micro-simulation of traﬃc system in other developing cities.
 2005 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
Keywords: Microscopic simulation; Modelling; Pedestrian; Traﬃc violation
1. Introduction
Traﬃc simulation has been widely used to evaluate control scenarios in recent years. Pedestrian is a so
important entity in traﬃc ﬂows that it could not be ignored when we lay a managing course. Nowadays, a
number of studies have been conducted on pedestrian simulation.
As for the microscopic aspect, several kinds of simulation model have been developed widely. Beneﬁt Cost
Cellular Model, Magnetic Force Model, and Social Force Model have been developed for a period of time.
These models mainly concern explanation of the phenomena of pedestrians queuing, way ﬁnding and self-
organization (Teknomo et al., 2000). In addition, cellular automata model has been introduced to provide
a microscopic description of pedestria

ns. For instance, bi-directional pedestrian movement was modeled based
on cellular automata by Blue and Adler (2001) and Fang et al. (2003).
0965-8564/$ - see front matter  2005 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
doi:10.1016/j.tra.2005.08.001
* Corresponding author. Tel.: +86 29 8266 8630 309.
E-mail address: yjg@mail.xjtu.edu.cn (J. Yang).
Transportation Research Part A 40 (2006) 280–290
www.elsevier.com/locate/tra

However, little information has been published concerning micro-simulation of pedestrians signal non-
compliance decision when they are crossing road. Researches on pedestrians violation mainly focus on soci-
ology and psychology to ﬁnd out which situational factors could have inﬂuence on pedestrians, and whether it
would be diﬀerent with diﬀerent age or gender.
A questionnaire was distributed to 205 Israeli college students. Respondents were asked to answer how the
presence of a certain factor, like traﬃc volume, weather, other pedestrians violation, would aﬀect their behav-
iors as crossings with a dont walk sign (Yagil, 2000). Hamed (2001) paid attention to the divided streets (with
refuge island). Impact of the pedestrian waiting countdown timer on pedestrian behavior was also studied
(Keegan and Mahony, 2003).
Liu et al. (2000) developed a micro-simulation tool for assessing pedestrian delays and traﬃc management.
They classiﬁed pedestrians into two types: ‘‘law-obeying’’ ones and ‘‘opportunistic’’ ones. But proportion of
each type was simply predetermined before simulation, not depended on the decision results of pedestrians
and varies along with the traﬃc conditions.

As a developing country, China has her own traﬃc characteristics. Researches show the proportion of
walking in the total egression comes to 40% in big cities in China, and it must be higher in smaller cities or
in towns. Owing to the weakness of safety consciousness and misgoverning, pedestrians violation in China
is popular. Report on the Investigation of Pedestrians Road-crossing Behavior in the Main Road of
Guangzhou in 2003 shows, in average, 23.3% of pedestrians took an illegal action while crossing streets,
and the rate came to 66.7% in certain sections. Most pedestrians always turn a blind eye to the traﬃc lights.
Consequently, the road is often under the situation that vehicles and pedestrians being inﬂuenced by each
other and the mixed traﬃc ﬂow has been a typical feature of the Chinese city traﬃc.
Due to the special features of Chinese city traﬃc, application of the micro-simulation tools developed by
foreign countries in China has not obtained obvious eﬀect. He (2003) once studied the applicability of COR-
SIM and SIMTRAFFIC for signalized intersections in Beijing, China, results show that their vehicle models
can achieve favorable eﬀect, but the pedestrian and bicycl

e models could not truly describe the situations at
intersections. Modeling of pedestrians behavior and its impact on traﬃc ﬂow is a key point need to be solved
in the ﬁeld of traﬃc system micro-simulation in our country.
Some researchers in China have realized this particularity, and correlative work is being done, but it still
needs a long way to go. Zhang et al. (2004) used cellular automata to derive a two-dimensional model for sim-
ulation of pedestrians crossing dynamics. Simulation of a crosswalk without signal was carried out. Interac-
tions between pedestrians and vehicles were described in some degree, but pedestrians ﬁghting for right of way
with vehicle drivers has not been fully presented. Gao et al. (2004) regarded pedestrians as a disturbance to
vehicle ﬂows. They observed a pedestrians crossing point in the city of Wuhan and brought forward a pedes-
trians disturbance model for traﬃc micro-simulation based on Poisson distribution. Parameters were cali-
brated by ﬁeld data. However, pedestrians crossing choices are seriously impacted by the current traﬃc
conditions, especially the vehicle gaps. Gao et al. have not taken these factors into consideration.
In simu

lation, it is an important thing to let the pedestrian know how to pass through the vehicle ﬂow
safely after their violation decisions. Many researchers have done investigations on this problem. In general,
Gap Acceptance is widely used as the judging criterion of pedestrians safety crossing. Each pedestrian is sup-
posed to have a critical gap, which is calculated by this equation (Wan and Rouphail, 2004)
CG ¼ L
S þ F
ð1Þ
where L is the crosswalk length, S is the pedestrians average walking speed. F is a safety factor reﬂects pedes-
trian aggressiveness. Pedestrians will do comparison between the vehicle gaps and the critical gap. The gap,
which is smaller than the critical gap, will be rejected. In 1991, Hunt and Griﬃths took two-direction vehicle
ﬂows into account and gave probability matrices for rejection of crossing opportunities for 10.0 m wide roads
(Lyons et al., 2001).
Appearance of the traﬃc jam in rush hours and the increase of accident rate have a strong relation to the
participators non-compliance. In order to truly reappear the traﬃc condition of the signalized intersections in
China, it is necessary to develop a model basing on human behavior for simulating ped

estrians red light
running when crossing roads.
J. Yang et al. / Transportation Research Part A 40 (2006) 280–290
281

This paper will give a description of a model establishment for simulating pedestrians road crossing behav-
iors in Chinese cities. In this model, pedestrians will completely make a decision whether run through red light
by them. Model parameters will be calibrated by the analysis of a questionnaire investigation of pedestrian
violation behavior at signalized intersections in the city of Xian, China, along with the videotape data. Com-
parison between simulation results and data extracted from videotape is given in the end.
2. Model framework
Nowadays, most intersections have equipped with a walk/dont walk sign for pedestrians in Chinese cities.
Nevertheless, since the lack for safety consciousness and eﬀective punishment, a majority of pedestrians do not
comply with the traﬃc lights. Red light running is popular. Once a proper gap occurs, pedestrians will go
through immediately. Furthermore, interactions among pedestrians themselves are serious. When someone
successfully cut in the gap between two vehicles, others will speed up to follow him/her and make the latter
vehicles yield to them. Since Road Traﬃc Safety Law was implemented on May 1st 2004, punishment for
pedestrians

violation has been aggravated. Consequently, proportion of pedestrians violation of a certain
road section may change with the strictness of the government of policeman.
We classiﬁed pedestrians into two types, according to their behavior during red light:
I. People who comply with the traﬃc law, and do not take an illegal action. Most elders belong to this type.
II. People who look for appropriate gaps between vehicles to cross during red man. Most pedestrians in
China could be considered as this type.
We hypothesize that if external factors, like policeman and other pedestrians behavior, have no eﬀect on a
pedestrian, his/her behavior during the red man will be relatively steady, and it only depends on his/her age,
morality and safety consciousness. In a large quantity of people, proportions of these two types of pedestrians
are constants.
In real life, humans decision-making and action process is very complex, and it is impacted and restricted
by many factors. Pedestrians behaviors may vary with external factors. For instance, some pedestrians of
Type II may become pedestrians of Type I, since they are afraid of being punished by the policeman. Some
pedestrians of Type I ma

y follow other pedestrians to jump the red light and become a member of pedestrians
of Type II. Due to the inﬂuences of all these external factors, traﬃc system becomes more complex, unpredict-
able, with strong randomness. If we only set the proportion of each pedestrian type before simulation, these
features could not be represented adequately.
According to the situation in Chinese cities, we ﬁnally choose policeman, behavior of other pedestrians and
vehicle ﬂow from all the external situational factors, while others will be ignored.
Fig. 1 shows the model framework. Pedestrians are generated according to the proportion of the two types
of pedestrians in general conditions. Denote r1 as the proportion of pedestrians of Type I. Considering the
impact of population ﬂuidity and strictness of execution of law, this proportion can be adjusted according
to object of simulation. Pedestrians of Type I will stop and wait for the green man when facing the red light,
while pedestrians of Type II will look for a proper gap of vehicles to cross through. A number of pedestrians of
Type I will follow other people to violate traﬃc signal if someone else jumps the red light while his/her waiting.

We denote this proportion as r2.
In our model, pedestrian takes a ‘‘one by one’’ mode to cross through vehicle lanes. At this time, pedestrian
only considers the time gaps between vehicles in the adjacent lane, and he/she would not care what happens in the
other lanes until reaching the end point of the adjacent lane. Here, deﬁne DT as the safety criterion of gap accep-
tance for pedestrians. Fig. 2 shows the method of calculating DT. When a pedestrian walk to the curbside, he/she
will estimate the time T1, which is needed for the ﬁrst oncoming vehicle in adjacent lane to arrive at his/her posi-
tion. Meanwhile, T2, which is the time needed for the pedestrian to reach the safe position (the ultimate position
where the pedestrian can just avoid collision with the oncoming vehicle), will be estimated by the pedestrian.
DT is the diﬀerence between T1 and T2, which can be calculated by Eq. (2)
DT ¼ T 1  T 2 ¼ D=V  ðw þ d0Þ=V ped
ð2Þ
282
J. Yang et al. / Transportation Research Part A 40 (2006) 280–290

Fig. 1. Model framework.
J. Yang et al. / Transportation Research Part A 40 (2006) 280–290
283

Here, D is the distance between the front edge of the oncoming vehicle and the pedestrian, w is the width of the
coming vehicle, d0 is the distance between the position where the pedestrian stands and the vehicles lateral
edge which is near the pedestrian (see Fig. 2). Considering that a pedestrian cannot exactly predict the future
action of the vehicle, we use the pedestrians estimation of the vehicles instantaneous speed V to calculate T1,
Vped is the average walking speed of pedestrians: 1.2 m/s.
In order to obtain the proportion (r3) of pedestrians who accept a certain DT, we use image-processing soft-
ware to extract large amount of samples from videotape. It will be introduced in the next section.
3. Parameter calibrations
From the model framework we can see there are three proportion parameters, r1, r2 and r3, need to be cal-
ibrated. Questionnaires and image processing technology are used to achieve this goal.
3.1. Questionnaires
In order to ﬁnd out characteristics of pedestrians behavior at intersections in Xian, questionnaires were
distributed from November 22nd to 23rd, 2003. Respondents were 200 students from Xian Jiaotong Univer-
sity and 300 pedestrians who were

chosen randomly and were willing to be investigated at the intersection
which lies on the northeast corner of Xian Jiaotong University (the conjunction of West Xianning Road
and Xingqing Road). Four hundred and ninety ﬁve valid answer sheets were reclaimed. 304 (61.4%) were male
and 191 (38.6%) were female. Table 1 shows the age distribution of these respondents.
The questions mainly focused on pedestrians behavior at intersections and the degree of the external sit-
uational factors (like policeman, traﬃc volume, weather, and other pedestrians behavior) work on probabil-
ities of pedestrians signal non-compliance. Questions, alternative answers and statistic results of the answers
are listed in Tables 2 and 3. Some questions may be skipped over by respondents, and these items were treated

as invalid ones that had not been counted in the percent calculating for the ﬁnal results. In addition, there was
an alternative answer ‘‘hard to say’’ in the question serials of the probabilities of pedestrians signal non-com-
pliance under speciﬁc situations. If a pedestrian had chosen this item, the corresponding question was also
treated as invalid, and it had no eﬀect on the ﬁnal results.
From these tables, we can see that more than half of the pedestrians do not have strong safety conscious-
ness while crossing roads. Some pedestrians even never care the state of traﬃc lights; say nothing of complying
with them. Among all the factors listed above, presence of other pedestrians who violate traﬃc signal, in a
hurry, low traﬃc volume and long duration of red light contributed more to the probabilities of pedestrians
signal non-compliance. What is noticeable and understandable is that presence of policeman did not have dis-
tinct impact on pedestrians. This may due to that, policemen had already spent much energy on the guidance
of vehicles, and their supervision towards pedestrians seemed to be powerless at that time.
Considering the pedestrians who chose the item ‘‘Minimum’’

as the one who were not going to violate traf-
ﬁc signal, in general conditions, this kind of pedestrians accounted for 55% (we use it as r1), while under the
circumstance that other pedestrians have violated traﬃc signal, this proportion reduced to 17%. Then, r2 is
calculated by this equation: r2 = 38/55 · 100% = 69%, it means that 69% of the pedestrians of Type I may fol-
low others to violate traﬃc signal. Since this part of pedestrians of Type I only become a red light jumper when
other pedestrians violate traﬃc signal, the ﬁnal proportion of red light running will ﬂuctuate between 45% and
83%. In this way, diﬀerences among various intersections will be represented.
3.2. Extracting DT from videotape
Calibrating parameters by the data extracted from videotape is a common method used in traﬃc simulation
research ﬁeld. Das et al. (2005) collected videotape data of three signalized intersections in New Delhi and
obtained pedestrians critical gap distribution of these intersections. They established an ordered probit model
of gap acceptance, which takes the conﬁguration of the intersection, pedestrians crossing point and attributes

J. Yang et al. / Transportation Research Part A 40 (2006) 280–290
285

of the pedestrian (such as age and gender) into account. Maximum likelihood method was used to estimate the
model parameter. However, the vehicle gaps are obtained by the diﬀerence of the time when the vehicle passed
the position where pedestrian stands. In fact, pedestrians could not exactly predict vehicles future actions at
the time when they decide whether to violate traﬃc signal. For this problem, we will use the pedestrians esti-
mation of the vehicles velocity to calculate the time that needed for the vehicle to reach the pedestrians posi-
tion. This is the diﬀerence between Das and Manskis method and that of ours. However, pedestrians
estimation of the vehicles velocity couldnt be extracted from the videotape, so we use the vehicles instanta-
neous speed to describe this velocity.
From Fig. 1 we can see pedestrians are supposed to be impacted by nothing but oncoming vehicles while
they passing through the gaps between vehicles after their signal non-compliance decisions. In order to obtain
pedestrians real behavior in this condition, we chose a zebra crossing without traﬃc lights, which is outside
the Southeast Gate of Xian Jiaotong University, as screening objec

tive. Video camera was placed on the 27th
ﬂoor of Ruisen Building (a building near the Southeast Gate of Xian Jiaotong University). Fig. 3 shows a
picture intercepted from the videotape. The zebra crossing lies in the middle. Areas lined out by white rectan-
gles are deﬁned as pedestrians waiting areas, since most of pedestrians in our country will stand in these areas
to wait for proper vehicle gaps to pass through. A pedestrian has to walk through four lanes until she/he reach
the non-motor area on the opposite side.
At ﬁrst, the video was saved as several ﬁles with fmpg format. Then, we used the continuously photograph-
ing function of a software tool named Hero3000 to get a serial of pictures. A video with the length of 1 s can
obtain 24 pictures, which have the same time interval.
A program was written to extract DT from the picture serials by us. From the time a pedestrian happen to
arrive at the waiting area to the time he/she reaches the waiting area on the opposite side of the road, once
when the pedestrian encountered a vehicle, DT was calculated. Hitting four points by mouse completed every
calculation of DT, including the position where the pedestrian stands, the crit

ical collision point between pe-
destrian and the oncoming vehicle, the vehicles current position, and the vehicles position 0.25 s later (the
program will automatically show this picture after hitting the former three points).
It is very important to choose the initiative picture for calculation. We use ACDSee 4.0 to scan all pictures and
complete this choosing process. For the ﬁrst vehicle a pedestrian encountered, the initiative picture correspond to
the time when the pedestrian happen to arrive at the waiting area, while for other vehicles, the initiative picture
correspond to the time when the vehicle in front of them passed over the position where the pedestrian stands.
Finally, 989 samples were collected. Fig. 4 shows the distribution of these samples, which was clustered by
whether the pedestrian had passed through the corresponding gap. Fig. 5 shows the relationship between DT
and the proportion of pedestrian that accept the corresponding gap.
Fig. 3. Zebra crossing outside the Southeast Gate of Xian Jiaotong University.
286
J. Yang et al. / Transportation Research Part A 40 (2006) 280–290

4. Simulation results
In order to simplify the vehicle model, turning vehicles has been considered as straight going. So the sim-
ulating scene becomes a transmeridional road section of an intersection, which has two directions and four
lanes, with one pavement on each side. Fig. 6 shows the road map.
Simulation program runs under the Visual C++ 6.0 circumstance. Before its running, we can set whether
the police are on duty in dialog box. Participators in the traﬃc simulation include vehicles and pedestrians.
Vehicles in each lane are generated by four car-generators separately. Headways of the vehicles in the same
lane are subject to M3 distribution. The simulation platform can count vehicle volume at two pavements,
and ‘‘pedestrians’’ could obtain it if they need, together with the police information. In addition, ‘‘pedestrian’’
can also get information about vehicles and other pedestrians to a certain range, then, they will make

decisions. We can see pedestrians are marking with three colors: white, black and grey, they represent pedes-
trians of Type I, Type II, the pedestrians who follow others to violate traﬃc signal.
From June 2003 to April 2004, our team members recorded the pedestrians activities at some crossings
without police on duty in the city of Xian by video camera. We extract information artiﬁcially by the recorder
explored by ourselves, including vehicle ﬂow rate, pedestrian ﬂow rate, arriving time and leaving time of each
pedestrian, the proportion of pedestrians violation and etc. We use them as reference criterion of the simu-
lation results. In addition, simulation results are extracted by these rules for the same items.
4.1. Pedestrians generation
Fig. 7 shows comparison of the distribution of pedestrians arriving intervals between the data extracted
from videotape and simulation results. We can see this distribution approximatively subject to M3 distribution
(except the original point). Since pedestrians may walk along with their friends or relatives, so the interval,
which equals to 0, accounts for a large proportion. So, simply use M3 distribution to describe pedestrians
arrivi

ng intervals could not truly represent real situation. We make some changes on the basis of M3 distri-
bution and acquire the simulation results.
4.2. Proportions of red light jumpers and violation followers
Pedestrians who leave the waiting area when the light is red are considered to be red light runners; while the
one who leave the waiting area when the light is red after other red light runners within 2 s are considered to be
violation followers. Proportion of red light jumpers is the ratio of red light jumpers to people who arrive at the
waiting area in red light period. Proportion of violation followers is the ratio of violation followers to red light
jumpers.
We use the phases of the traﬃc lights, vehicle ﬂow rates, and pedestrian volumes as the simulation input
parameters. Proportion of pedestrian of Type I(r1), proportion of violation followers in pedestrian of Type
I(r2), and proportion of pedestrians accept a certain DT (r3) are set according to calibrated parameters. The
ﬁrst group of simulation results is acquired under this parameter setting.
Tables 4 and 5 show the comparison of proportions of red light jumpers and violation followers between
data extracted from vide

otape and simulation results in proportions of red light jumpers and violation follow-
ers. We can see that proportion of red light jumpers in simulation results acquired from calibrated parameters
is much lower than the data extracted from videotape, while proportion of violation followers is much higher.
This may due to the degree of conﬁdence of the result of questionnaire. The objects of this investigation are
the pedestrians who were willing to complete this questionnaire, and they could not represent all pedestrians.
Furthermore, answers provided by some pedestrians are not true response what they do in their daily life. We

adjusted parameter r2 to 0.35 and acquired the second group of simulation results. From Tables 4 and 5, we
can see simulation results are more approximate to the data extracted from videotape.
5. Conclusion
We have established a model of pedestrians road-crossing behavior, which includes the eﬀect of policeman,
other pedestrians and vehicles. It represents the behavior of two types of pedestrians (law-obeying ones and
opportunistic ones) when facing red light. From the simulation results, violation followers behavior and phe-
nomenon which vehicles and pedestrians being inﬂuenced by each other are represented, and the mixed traﬃc
characteristics are also considered.
Questionnaires and data extracted from videotape are used to calibrate model parameters. However, the
simulation results using calibrated parameters deviate from the data extracted from videotape. By adjusting
them on the basis of analyzing the occurrence of the deviations, the simulation results agree with ﬁeld results
better.
However, the current work only models pedestrians decision making of red light running according to the
instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles. What pedestrians will do when oncoming vehicl

e change their
states (e.g. accelerate, decelerate, etc) still need to be studied. In addition, the calculation method of DT needs
to be improved for the turning vehicles so that the model can be more practical for simulating Chinese city
traﬃc.
References

321, 633–640.

Economy in Area of Communications 4, 58–60 (in Chinese).
